"All who engage in this art [of alchemy], and are lacking the knowledge and perception of things which the philosophers have described in their books, are erring immensely; for the philosophers have founded this art in a natural beginning, but of a very hidden operation."

comments.title: Moral Behavior
comments.content:
What will be the moral behavior during the following situation?

In the future a family relation (Doe) is going to become bedridden due to a lifetime of resistance to physical activity and a highly inflammatory diet. Due to me being the only person in the family in the 10-60 year old range, and out of a sense of obligation, I will be the individual providing care. Doe will probably become verbally and emotionally abusive if I not provide constant access to sweets and entertainment. I know my motivations matter, but is there any obligation to provide that which is harmful? Most of Doe’s physical ailments could be ameliorated or fully reversed by a healthy diet and lack of television.

Other details:
Doe occasionally cared for me as a child, and is generally nice.
Doe has zero assets, there is a negative financial incentive to providing care.
Doe has a very robust cognitive denial tendency.

Unfortunately, I have a modest emotional desire to provide only fresh vegetables, fruits, and meats, as a way of punishing Doe for their lack of care for themselves.

replies.title: Re: Moral Behavior
replies.content: I'm not a moral philosopher or an ethicist, you know, and this question has nothing to do with occultism, so I'm going to pass.

replies.title: Re: Moral Behavior
replies.content: Sorry for being off-topic, and thank you for your response.

replies.title: Re: Moral Behavior
replies.content:
I'm going to butt in here and chime in as a healthy, cook-from-scratch ten year ethical vegan (sooo much fiber!) with elderly, unhealthy, meat-eating, TV watching parents. I used to get irritated with them for their eating habits especially when I lived with them for a three year stint in my 40s. I'd always smell whatever junky Arby's crap they were eating from downstairs and know it was making them suffer as well as perpetuating the egregious practices of factory farming. They both have fairly serious chronic health problems.

My advice: just let Doe eat the garbage and be inactive and don't bother yourself about it. Let's put it this way: I won't go out of my way to buy my parents a meat product, but if I'm picking up takeout for dinner, I tell them to order whatever they want even though it's usually meaty junk food. I buy my mom, who as I said has a chronic health condition, chocolates and giant cinnamon rolls. I make my dad oatmeal raisin cookies that are mostly butter and sugar. He often eats the whole batch in three days. Everyone's got to die of something and basically it is not my job to improve their habits in this lifetime. When my parents do make an effort to eat well or to be active, no matter how small or faulty or small that effort is, I praise them. When I was living with them, I made vegan lunch every weekday and sometimes weekends too, it inspired them to eat a little better once I left and moved into my own house. If my parents get a stomach ache from say, eating 40 oatmeal raisin cookies in 3 days, that's their responsibility and I think they know better than to moan to me about it! If I punished them and only brought them steamed carrots, they would feel more entitled to complain, I think.

replies.title: Re: Moral Behavior
replies.content: I like carrots AND Arby’s steamed beef!  But we have the carrots a lot more often than the beef—Arby’s is quite expensive, for one thing, and also Sonkitten doesn’t like steamed beef so if I wanted Arby’s, I’d have to get or make him something else.  The upshot is that on the once-in-a-blue-moon occasions when I’m out by myself I stop at Arby’s.  I feel like a Victorian gentleman sneaking off to the local house of the rising sun.  🙄

replies.title: Re: Moral Behavior
replies.content:
I agree with Kimberly,
Your relative is an adult and I think might respond pretty well to shared meals that start with a nice crunchy salad and then something you know they will like. When I made my city family a couple of salads as sides for a lunch I was surprised that they ate every crunchy morsel and really enjoyed it.

Also, I find if you cut up apples and pears every slice will disappear fast, much more so than putting a whole fruit out. Same is true for healthful snacks such as carrot and celery sticks. Let your relative eat them with some tasty dip. I am sure they will enjoy them.
Maxine

replies.title: Re: Moral Behavior
replies.content: Kimberly, thank you for your response. Your perspective reminded me which ideals I will keep.

replies.title: Re: Moral Behavior
replies.content:
If I may. I suspect that if this were a question about how to relate to a god or a spirit, JMG would have turned his Magic 8-ball to the standard answer - "Ask them".

This is a human being, and yet, I reckon the same ethics apply. Why not ask them?

:)

[earthworm]

replies.title: Re: Cat pee magic
replies.content:
The species you are a member of is social; dogs are also a social species, and run in packs in nature. Cats in the wild are definitely solitary and territorial. So perhaps you’re viewing your cats through an “all species are social” filter.

All three species I mention have, relatively speaking, dominant and submissive individuals. My personal experience with cats is that you cannot have two dominant cats in the same household. One dominant and one submissive (or perhaps two) works fine, though the Alpha cat will kick the behind of the submissive cat(s) occasionally just to remind the beta cat(s) of who is the boss. I introduced a small Burmese female into my house that already had a large male Abyssinian; the Abby male (Spirit) quickly learned to always defer in all ways to tiny Maya. That’s how cats work.

So when you trim your Herd of Cats (and as other posters have indicated, that’s likely your only solution to the pee problem), study them closely to see who defers to whom. The signs are subtle and oftentimes very brief. Finding another home for the most-dominant cat(s) will allow you to retain more cats, if that is what you wish. If it turns out that the most-dominant cat is your favorite, then ahh, you have a dilemma.

Best wishes!

replies.title: Re: Cat pee magic
replies.content: I volunteer with a local animal shelter and foster some of the really scared ones. Giving them their own space seems to be very important. While once they have acclimated to the household they can get used to using a communal litter box, having their own private one helps at first. How many boxes do you have? Best recommendation is one more than the number of cats.

replies.title: Re: Cat pee magic
replies.content:
I have the total cats plus one boxes.

Love your questing, by the way....

replies.title: Re: Cat pee magic
replies.content:
On a more practical note:

1) Is one of the cats an "unfixed" tomcat? These will pee in random places to mark territory.

2) If not (1), how clean is the litter box? (Or litter boxes, if you have multiple.) If it's too full of "stuff", the cats will relieve themselves elsewhere.

I once had a housemate with one cat who picked out the cat's litter box daily. That cat, a fixed tom, never went to the bathroom elsewhere.

On the other hand, when my Dad had three cats, he did not pick out the litter box nearly often enough. Every time I visited, it was full of poo and pee-clods and I had to swamp it out and put a bunch of new litter in it. Those cats went to the bathroom all over the rest of the house.

My recommendation is that each of your litter box(es) should not accumulate more than one pee clod and poo per day. If you have to add litter boxes and pick them out daily, so be it. You didn't say how many cats you have, so I can't quote numbers beyond that.

- Cicada Grove

replies.title: Re: Cat pee magic
replies.content:

replies.title: Re: Cat pee magic
replies.content:
I had a similar problem once many years ago when one cat decided the litter box was his territory and actively defended if from the other cats. You may just have to provide more boxes.
Currently have three cats, and one big box (those underbed plastic storage boxes that are long enough to take up an entire bathtub.) yep, the cats get the 2nd bathroom in the back that we never use. There were four cats when this was originally set up. They are all allowed access to outdoors thru a kitty door (locked in at night for safety- raccoons, you know!) The oldest would come inside, use the box, then go back out. Two of the others would use it only when locked in, and the 4th would just hold it in until the door was opened in the morning. The oldest is now gone (congestive heart failure not just a human thing!) and all three have decided they prefer outside for doing their business. There is one that will use it occasionally if the weather is bad. And the one that always preferred to hold it still does. Even if cold and windy and rainy, he will slip outside, do his business then come back in.
All this to say- cats are quirky creatures and if they’re peeing outside the box, they’re telling you they’re not happy with their toileting arrangements.
Best of luck to you in your attempts to work it out!
Orange Exiguous Piglet

replies.title: Re: Cat pee magic
replies.content:
The only thing I can think of that hasn’t been mentioned is, do you have a lot of cats (in general, not a lot of litter box problem cats)? In general, cats don’t like crowding, and as someone else mentioned, they may pee on the floor to let you know they’re unhappy about something.

replies.title: Re: Cat pee magic
replies.content: Jackson Galaxy would say they are upset about something, because happy cats usually do not act like that. Marking territory is one way they deal with insecurity, and they are trying to get some control on their surroundings. They might have an antagonistic relationship with each other that is not immediately apparent, or there could be something in the environment that is setting it off. Cats are very sensitive creatures, which makes them a great early warning system for unwelcome energy.

comments.title: Meditation with Pen and Paper
comments.content:
Separate question, I ran across the AODA website, and your "A Druid Meditation Primer". That was helpful. I also read Mam Adar's "A Few Thoughts on Discursive Meditation" where Adar talked about written meditation.

"In the evening, before I settle down to go to sleep, I read a bit of “Wisdom from the Stone Circle”, until something grabs my attention. Then, I open up my notebook and write a cluster about it, putting the topic in a circle in the center of the page and letting other ideas branch out from it. The resulting mass of circles, lines, and words resembles a flowchart gone amok. I leave my notebook open and my pen handy so that I can record my dreams when I wake, and then I sleep on it, letting the nonconscious parts of my mind work on the meditation material. In the morning, I have time to review what I wrote before setting out to walk to work."

I have to say that method really appeals to me. I'm very visually orientated and love diagraming out problems I have on paper. My office has one whole wall with scraps of paper on it with various notes and ideas.

I'm guessing that isn't a method you use, but do you have any comments or observations about it. I'm still trying the sit in a chair method but since I've been keeping a journal for a while now, I thought the two processes would work well together, especially since I also take a morning walk 5 or so days a week.

replies.title: Re: Meditation with Pen and Paper
replies.content: The processes would work very well together. I don't use written meditation as such, but my writing process has some things in common with it!

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hi John,
A few weeks back I asked what a binding spell was and whether that might be an appropriate means for changing my own habit of not being orderly. You didn't say what the definition of a binding spell was but suggested I begin using the willpower training program on changing my habits. I did and I think I am going on three weeks now of focusing 3+ hours a day with not a single day missed. I'm not entirely sure why I'm able to do this well now when before I kept failing miserably ... nor how long I will be able to keep it up. But it's one day at a time and I am happy for every day I move past my blockage. Some of the change, I think, is giving myself more flexibility in how I allocate my time but just focusing on doing something -- anything -- legitimately willful one hour at a time and not beating myself up if I don't do things exactly according to my plan. (So some days I do less organizing, and more of other willful things, and some days I do a lot more). But just the commitment to try to follow your outline as an choice rather than an obligation has a lot to do with it as well. And then there is the matter of paying more attention to how I feel as I go about making my willful choices.

I remain curious about what constitutes a binding spell and would appreciate any guidance or pointing to a source you might give. I'm just curious, as the only definitions I've been able to find on line pertain to keeping a person away from something...not really binding them to anything (so calling it a binding doesn't seem quite right).

Are you aware of -- or have you ever written about -- an overall map of fits into the domain of magic? That map of magic might list categories of spells or it might be in some other form, but I think it might be helpful. And maybe it is already in one of your books or prior articles? If not, maybe you would consider addressing my question in the future?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Delighted to hear that the will training is doing it for you. Binding spells are almost always binding someone not to do something. You're literally tying someone up so they can't do something. It's not something to do casually -- I've done such spells maybe three times in my life, and only in serious situations such as keeping an abusive boyfriend away from his victim, at her request.

An overall map of what goes where in the domain of magic would be an immense project, the kind of thing you'd want to assign to a large group of experienced mages rather than one writer! I don't know of anyone who's attempted it.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Do such bindings also have an effect on others that the abuser or stalker is trying to use for his purposes? For example people he tells to ask the victim to talk to him, or people that he has told bad things about her so that they are on his side and believe she is a bad person and he is right?

This is theoretical as it is years since such a thing happened to me, and now everyone I know realises the guy was a bad lot, without spells, though I know it does not always work out that way.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: It depends entirely on how the intention of the spell is phrased. "...or harm her in any way" was a bit of intention I always included in such spells, with good results.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Not the OP but isn't there a negative blowback to that kind of spell in terms of the "strawberry jam" principle ? If someone is using those energies to limit or restrict someone's range of actions, can they also bleed over in their own life in some ways ?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Of course. If you cast such a spell you need to accept exactly the same binding on yourself. If you're a decent human being, of course, binding yourself not to stalk, harrass, abuse, or rape another person isn't exactly a burden, but if you make accepting that binding on yourself explicit as part of the working, it adds a great deal of force to the spell.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: That makes sense ! Are those kind of spells usually given at a certain grade in magical systems, like at at a high degree in golden dawn, or are mages supposed to design them themselves using the knowldege they found through experience and study of occult philosophy ?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I've never seen one of any quality written down. Once you know your way around advanced ritual work, though, it's easy to construct one.

replies.title: Re: Geomancy reading and strange things going on in nature
replies.content:
Are you certain they were spring crocuses? Okay, a natal chart has to be read as a whole, not by picking it apart into individual placements. (That's just as true for other astrological charts, of course). replies.title: Re: Tarot deck
replies.content:
I’m not JMG, but I’ve been reading Tarot for forty-mumble years. I suggest learning from the Rider-Waite deck or any of its copycats like the Mary Hanson Roberts deck, because each of them has a picture on each card associated with the meaning. replies.title: Re: Tarot deck
replies.content:

2) See the answer above. Choose the deck that makes you say, "Wow, that's gorgeous."

3) I recommend Eden Gray's Mastering the Tarot as a beginning manual. Most decks also have an LWB ("little white booklet") inside, but most of those aren't very useful.

replies.title: Re: Recharging Energy
replies.content: It's a common practice among Druids and many people do it every day. Taoists also practice something closely related to it, and daily practice there is also common. replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Hi there,

I actually asked a similar question about the magical properties of dance over a year ago. The leads I received were very helpful in learning more about dance history in general, and how physical culture, yoga, and the occult developed in detail.

I'd recommend looking up Genevieve Stebbins as a good starting place.

Also, for what it is worth, I have researched a little bit into different folk dances in Europe. My main interest is in what direction the dancers move around the dance floor. In dances that predate coupled pairs, the direction is almost always in a clockwise direction. In the same direction as the sun. Dion Fortune mentions something similar to this in her book on Psychic Self-Defense at one point... although forgive me as the exact quote fails me on this occasion.

As far as I can tell, it was with the coming of partnered dances, such as the polka, that direction of the dancers changed to counter-clockwise around the dance floor. Of course that being said, the pairs still rotate clockwise within their partnership. In the Czech Republic, I have heard that teachers tell their polka student to "spin like the planets."

Bringing this back to magic, I have thought a lot about clockwise vs counter-clockwise spin. I think a model of the solar system and the heavenly bodies is useful in thinking about the movement of dancers on a dance floor. My suspicion is that they also relate to how the solar and telluric currents flow, although I am still early in exploring this.

I will note, however, that here in Krakow there is a park that surrounds the old town. Perhaps 1 in 10 trees have a twist to them, and the vast majority of these twists are counter-clockwise. There are also some powerful alignments based upon mounds here in Krakow. I've wondered how it is all related.

Does anyone else have data on how/why trees twist as they grow?

Cheer,

Greg
Poland

replies.title: (no subject)

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Hi Greg,
From an injury avoidance while dancing tango perspective, couples are typically in an embrace with the leads left hand extended and right cheek towards their partner. Where this configuration comes from I am not sure but handedness may play a part.

Couples dancing counter clockwise gives the best visibility of the floor for avoiding collisions with other couples. Stilettos can be effective weapons! Switching to a clockwise direction around the floor would likely mean needing to switch the embrace and relative head positions to maintain visibility.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
My guess is the magical properties vary somewhat by the dance. Argentine Tango is the only dance with which I have experience so there is likely to be both big and small differences with other styles.

Some aspects of tango which lend to magical potential:

Communication between partners is primarily through contact points in the chest/torso. At times I can feel the heart beat of my partner and syncing breathing is easy to do. There is also a physical energetic linking which can happen between partners and become pretty intense even with complete strangers.

In social Argentine Tango prescribed patterns linking the various techniques are not relied on by partners. The dance experience is very in the moment and unpredictable beyond a step or two. Being able to respond timely requires continuous physical relaxation and focus, creating a flow state between the partners and the music. (Music is said to be the third partner.)

The effect can extend further between couples in the group flow on the floor.

Partners who have closed their eyes dancing with me have often described a floating sensation with a loss of sense of time and place. As the lead I keep my eyes open but when I have been in the follower role have experienced the same.

I feel there is lots of potential for achieving magic goals beyond the few experiments I have done. The biggest difficulty at this time is social distancing and tango are incompatible.

replies.content: A fair amount. They're called larvae -- that word was used for them before the scientists borrowed it for baby insects. In terms of our perceptions of space, they're between three and six inches across and up to a foot long, pale, blobby, and more or less featureless. comments.title: Cooking and Compiling
comments.content:
Salutations JMG!

1) In The Celtic Golden Dawn you mention how many traditional recipes adhered to the elemental balancing scheme, and you give the example of beef served with mustard/horseradish. I tried mustard on beef steak (grainy stoneground mustard, not that yellow toothpaste that comes out of a Heinz bottle) and it was really good. Where might I find more such simple recipes with balanced elements? I struggle to cook for myself since most recipes always require huge numbers of uncommon ingredients and long prep times which feels overwhelming, and I was hoping there might be a "Simple Traditional Cooking" book that uses simple combinations of meat/eggs with vegetables and adheres to a balanced elemental structure.

2) Since I asked you about a magical syllabus for the aspiring mage I've been working off and on to make a university level practicum for the aspiring occultist. It started as trying to create a schedule around my own studies and after a while I realized that with a few tweaks to allow greater freedom it could be applied more broadly to anybody looking to take up occult practice. The organizational structure takes heavily from the Celtic Golden Dawn and the Druid Revival, since everything is organized around the Three Rays. I experimented with using the Tree of Life and the Nine Noble Virtues, but the Three Rays are much more flexible and less partisan in my opinion while allowing plenty of symbolism to guide the organizational structure. The Central Ray is left mostly blank because while the Right Ray is Above/Heavens/Force and the Left Ray is Below/Earth/Form, the Central Ray runs through the practitioner themselves and requires personal involvement. This pattern is carried to the central Rays of both Right and Left, they contain subjects that get you personally involved. Each Ray splits into three more Rays, each of which carries the same symbolism, but coloured by its parent ray. Both Right Rays advocate studying a field of magic, but the Rightmost is Astrology which is abstract and takes years to master, while the Leftmost is Natural Magic which you can get started on immediately. Both Left Rays advocate learning science, but the Leftmost ray is the ecology, botany, animal husbandry, hydrology, geology and meteorology of the area you live in, whereas the Rightmost Left Ray is more abstract science such as Chemistry, Anatomy, Mathematics, etc. The rays of the Central Ray are mostly blank, except it's recommended to fill the Centre-Centre ray with a magical training course, though the rays of the Central ray are flexible if you wanted to simply practice Divination, Discursive Meditation and Daily Ritual instead of a set course. My goals have shifted from making a schedule for a series of reading lists to creating a guide somebody can follow to receive a "University-level Occult Education." Essentially the guide I wish I could send my past 2017 self to cut out all the worthless twaddle I wasted time and money on.

This is a rendered image of the work-in=progress project file I have on my computer: https://cubeupload.com/im/BoulderChum/trainingcourse1.png I'd appreciate any criticism and suggestions from you good sir and any other members who manage to see this. What's taking me a long time (besides my inability to sleep longer that 4 hours and the ever-present brain fog for the past three weeks) is compiling the Occult Reading List of the Right-Centre ray, which is supposed to contain every influential occult book ever, and finding reliable books to teach yourself things like Chemistry and Electromagnetic Physics starting from the most simple levels. It's hard to know which high school text books have been dumbed down and difficult to find university text books that aren't too expensive and don't assume the professor will be unpacking one page at a time over multiple semesters.

replies.title: Re: Cooking and Compiling
replies.content:
(Not JMG)

The best place to get a decent cookbook is the used bookstore. Look for one put out by a big business targeting women—Betty Crocker, Better Homes And Gardens, Good Housekeeping—or for The Joy Of Cooking: in all cases, get the oldest one available. We have Mom’s 1948 GH cookbook. It talks about what to do when you acquire a fridge—many people still had iceboxes then. Not only will it teach you to cook, it’s a fascinating historical document. 1980 is a good cut-off period—books published later than that tend to be full of ingredients that were faddish at the time of publication and short on basic how-to.

replies.title: Re: Cooking and Compiling
replies.content:
1) I don't know of a good elemental cookbook, but any standard cookbook from before 1960 will focus on traditional recipes and meal combinations.

2) As for the syllabus, it's usually not a good idea to try to figure out what advanced training is like before you've done it! Once you've finished the Druid Grade curriculum you'll have a better sense of what's useful and what's not.

replies.title: Re: Cooking and Compiling
replies.content: If I may - a lot of churches and community groups publish their own cookbooks, which are made up of members' contributions. Since most of these members are usually women who have raised children, the recipes tend towards the simple and effective. Recipes may be meat- and carbohydrate- heavy, since there was a lot more manual labour going on in earlier generations.

replies.title: Re: Cooking and Compiling
replies.content:
Also, I forgot to mention the old Farm Journal cookbooks edited by Nell B. Nichols. Cooking For Company, in particular, tells you how to cook game.

—Lady Cutekitten

replies.title: Re: Cooking and Compiling
replies.content:
In both traditional Chinese medicine and Ayurveda, both a complete meal and a complete dish will contain balanced amounts of all the elements aka flavors. This is thought to result in good tasting food, good digestion, and good health. I've read this in quite a few cookbooks on those cuisines, as well as hearing this from martial artists, yogis, etc... If you consider most recipes, from any tradition, you'll see this principle at work.

I'm not a fancy cook, but I know my way around the kitchen,and applying this principle has always made whatever I'm working on better. Healing With Whole Foods, by Paul Pitchford, is a classic hippie cookbook that gets deep into balancing flavors and elements. It's a bit old fashioned and leans toward macrobiotic/Japanese/Chinese concepts and ingredients, but I've found the general framework to be very flexible and useful.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
There was a time in American history when there was a very, very widespread terror of being buried alive; and there seems to have been enough evidence (from reburials, exhumatione, etc.) that this was a quite reasonable fear at the time. One of the results was the rise of cremation; another was requirements of embalming.

Also, at least here in the non-Puritan parts of New England, there was a long-lived folk tradition about the dead returning from their graves to kill off the still living members of their families. One of the local folk-remedies for this (here in Rhode Island) was to exhume the graves of your dead family members, look for undecayed (or otherwise deviant) corpses, cut out the hearts from those corpses and burn them, put the ashes of the burned hearts into water (or some other potable), and give it to all the living members of the family to drink. This was practiced for over a century, from the late 1700s into the late 1800s, or even the early 1900s. It was generally done with some degree of secrecy, but a surprisingly large number of cases (a couple of hundred, IIRC) have left traces nationwide in surviving town records and old newspapers. Not all of them are from New England; some come from other parts of the USA that were settled out of the non-Puritan parts of New England.

Michael E. Bell has written an excellent book on this: "Food for the Dead" (2nd ed., 2011). He has had a sequel in progress for some years now, but it still hasn't appeared.

replies.title: Scribd
replies.content:
(not a question)

Scribd has had a paid subscription service for several years. Many of your books are within this, added by the publishers. It is like Spotify (music service) for books, though with a less comprehensive selection

If you do a search on Scribd, titles listed under "Books" are legitimately uploaded by publishers and are part of the subscription service. Items listed under "Documents" are not, those are user-uploaded. There is a lot less pirated whole-book material than there used to be on Scribd, in my experience. Many of the newer uploaded documents are material from the web, journals or isolated chapters or background material to current affairs and other topics

replies.title: Re: Scribd
replies.content: Thanks! I'd been under the impression that publishers uploaded the books in the "books" section as well, which is why I assumed it was legit. That said, I know lots of musicians are not exactly thrilled with spotify's rather predatory terms, and I could imagine this being similar. If our host would rather not have his books accessed in this way, I'll take him at his word, and will finish reading the druidry handbook via a copy from the library (this gave me an incentive to finally learn how to check out ebooks from the library, which I'd been meaning to figure out for some time, so I'm not displeased by the result).
